name: The burial of Jesus
notes: All four Gospels record the burial of Jesus by Joseph of Arimathaea, and the shared core runs in close parallel: Joseph goes to Pilate and begs the body, takes it down and wraps it in linen, and lays it in a new tomb hewn out of rock. The accounts give complementary vantages on Joseph himself, naming him a rich man (Matthew), an honourable counsellor who waited for the kingdom of God (Mark, Luke), and a disciple secretly for fear of the Jews (John). Matthew and Mark note the great stone rolled to the door; Luke and John explicitly say the tomb was one wherein never man before was laid, which Matthew complements as Joseph's own new tomb. Each account also keeps unique matter that reads in place: Mark records Pilate marvelling that Jesus was already dead and questioning the centurion; John recounts Nicodemus bringing myrrh and aloes about an hundred pound weight; and Luke follows the women as they prepared spices and ointments and rested the sabbath according to the commandment.
